summary: Gruber dismantles analyst Ezra Gottheil's prediction that Apple will announce a $599 netbook at Macworld Expo. He argues that netbooks aren't a distinct category but merely cheap laptops, that MacBook sales remain strong despite the recession, and that Steve Jobs explicitly said Apple won't make a $500 computer. He further notes that Apple would never pre-announce laptops six months before selling them, since that would cannibalize existing MacBook sales — unlike the iPhone, which had no predecessor to protect.
key_points: Netbooks are not a genuine new category — they're just cheap laptops running XP or Linux instead of Vista
MacBook sales were actually up significantly, especially the new aluminum models, undermining the premise that Apple needed a cheaper option
Steve Jobs explicitly stated Apple doesn't know how to make a $500 computer that isn't junk, making a $599 netbook prediction contradictory
Apple would never pre-announce laptops six months out because it would cannibalize existing MacBook sales — the iPhone pre-announcement only worked because Apple had no existing phone to protect
Apple doesn't need to serve the low-end market any more than BMW needs to sell $12,000 sedans
money_quotes: What is the difference between a 'netbook' and a 'really cheap laptop that runs something other than Vista'?
The whole idea is predicated on the assumption that MacBook sales are in the tank — that the economy is in the shitter and the new MacBook lineup is priced too high. But that's only half right.
Mac laptop sales are up up up, especially the new aluminum MacBooks.
Apple doesn't need to serve this market any more than BMW needs to serve the market for $12,000 sedans.
The only reason anyone pre-announces any product is in the hope that customers will wait for it.
Apple is already in the business of selling laptop computers, so they do not and will not announce new ones until they are ready, or nearly ready, to sell them.
key_insight: Apple's strong MacBook sales and explicit refusal to compete on price make a $599 netbook prediction baseless, and the proposed pre-announcement strategy contradicts Apple's established product launch logic.
